title: Providing for social media platforms and for limiting censorship.
last_action: Referred to CONSUMER PROTECTION, TECHNOLOGY AND UTILITIES
llm_rationale: This bill primarily requires social media platforms to publish acceptable use policies, notify users of content removals, provide an appeal mechanism, and publish biannual transparency reports. While the appeal process (Sections 5-6) could result in removed content being restored to a user's feed, this is an indirect and case-by-case mechanism rather than a systemic change to feed ranking or personalization. The bill does not restrict algorithmic ranking, require chronological feeds, limit data used for personalization, or otherwise directly alter what users see in their feeds — the causal link to feed content is weak and mediated by individual appeals.

Consumer Data Protection Act; social media platforms, addictive feed.
last_action: Left in Communications, Technology and Innovation
llm_rationale: The bill defines 'addictive feed' and 'addictive social media platform' in Virginia's Consumer Data Protection Act, establishing legal categories for algorithmic feeds that personalize content based on user data. Section 59.1-577.1 (added by the bill but truncated) appears to impose obligations on platforms offering addictive feeds, and the definitional framework carves out permissible uses (e.g., user-selected settings, explicit user requests) while restricting others, which would constrain how platforms can rank and recommend content. The bill's primary purpose is specifically to regulate algorithmic feed personalization on social media platforms, making it a high-tier bill; however, confidence is moderate because the operative provisions in §59.1-577.1 are truncated and the full scope of user-facing controls or platform obligations cannot be fully assessed from the available text.

Trade: business practices; regulation of social media use by minors; provide for. Creates new act.
llm_rationale: Section 5 requires social media companies to verify the age of applicants and existing account holders, and to obtain parental consent before minors can hold accounts. This changes the content environment for minors by preventing them from accessing personalized feeds without parental consent, and by blocking access entirely if verification criteria are not met. However, the bill does not directly regulate ranking signals, recommendation algorithms, or feed personalization mechanics — the feed impact is indirect, flowing from the gatekeeping mechanism rather than explicit feed-design requirements. The parent exercises control over whether the minor has any account access at all, making this a parental control scenario consistent with the 'medium' tier.

Relating to statutory damages in actions brought by social media users against social media platforms for prohibited censorship.
llm_rationale: This bill amends Section 143A.007 of the Texas Civil Practice and Remedies Code to add statutory damages ($750–$30,000 per violation) as a remedy when a social media platform is found to have engaged in 'prohibited censorship' of a user. While this does not directly control feed ranking or personalization, the threat of statutory damages for content censorship could indirectly affect what content platforms allow to remain visible in users' feeds, creating a deterrent effect on content removal or suppression decisions. However, the bill's mechanism is litigation-based rather than a direct regulatory mandate on feed design, making the causal link to feed content weak and indirect.

title: Social media: warning labels.
last_action: Chaptered by Secretary of State - Chapter 671, Statutes of 2025.
llm_rationale: AB 56 requires covered platforms to display mandatory black box warning messages to users under 18 at initial daily access, after 3 hours of cumulative use, and hourly thereafter. These warnings appear within the platform interface and interrupt use, but they do not change the content, ranking, or personalization of the feed itself — they only add a mandatory health warning overlay. While the warnings are displayed 'in feed' in the sense that they interrupt platform use, they do not alter what content is recommended, how it is ranked, or what data is used for personalization, placing this bill at 'low' rather than 'medium' since no actual feed content or ranking mechanism is modified.

Social media-parental consent for minors required.
llm_rationale: This bill requires parental consent before a minor can create an account on a social media platform, effectively gate-keeping minors' access to any feed content whatsoever. Under W.S. 40-32-102(a) and (b), platforms must deny access until parental consent is verified, which materially affects what minors see by conditioning their entire feed experience on parental approval. Per the parental controls note, the minor is not exercising control — a parent or guardian is — so this is classified as 'medium' rather than 'high,' as the feed impact is mediated through parental consent rather than direct platform-side restrictions on feed content or ranking.

South Carolina Social Media Regulation Act
llm_rationale: The bill establishes an Age-Appropriate Code Design framework (Chapter 80) that directly regulates how covered online services may interact with minors, including restricting 'covered design features' such as infinite scroll, autoplay, algorithmic recommendation systems, and gamification elements. The definition of 'personalized recommendation system' (Section 39-80-10(12)) and 'expressed preferences' explicitly constrain how platforms may rank or recommend content to minors, requiring that recommendations be based on genuine user preferences rather than engagement-maximizing signals. Additionally, provisions requiring age verification, restricting access during certain hours, and prohibiting dark patterns would materially alter the default feed environment for minor users, though control is exercised at the platform and parental level rather than by the minor directly. The bill text is truncated, so some provisions governing specific obligations may not be fully visible, moderately reducing confidence.

llm_rationale: This bill materially affects what minor users see in their feeds through multiple mechanisms. Section 149-e explicitly prohibits social media companies from using 'targeted or suggested groups, services, products, posts, accounts, or users' for minor account holders, directly eliminating algorithmic personalization and recommendation from their feeds. Additionally, Section 149-e prohibits advertising display and restricts data collection to only what is legally necessary, removing the data inputs that drive feed personalization. Section 149-d requires age verification and parental consent before a minor can hold an account, functioning as a gatekeeping mechanism. The primary purpose of the bill is regulation of minors' social media experience, making this a 'high' tier classification — the feed impact is platform-side (platform must reconfigure how it serves content to minors) rather than user-controlled, and a parent exercises the consent rather than the minor.

title: Providing for social media platforms and for limiting censorship.
llm_rationale: This bill (the Social Media Anti-Censorship Act) primarily regulates platform content moderation transparency and appeals processes. Section 5 requires platforms to notify users when content is removed and allow appeals, and Section 7 mandates biannual transparency reports including data on content deprioritization (Section 7(a)(2)(iii)). While the appeal process could result in content being restored to feeds, and the reporting requirement covers 'content deprioritization,' there is no direct mechanism giving users control over algorithmic ranking or feed personalization — the bill addresses censorship/removal processes rather than feed algorithm design. The causal link to what users see in their feeds is plausible but indirect, making this a low-tier classification.

title: Social media use by minors.
llm_rationale: The bill (IC 24-16) requires social media services to obtain written parental/guardian consent before allowing Indiana minor residents to hold accounts, and requires platforms to configure minor accounts 'in a specified manner' — language that implies mandatory default settings changes affecting what minors see. Age verification and parental consent gates would materially change the content environment for minors, including feed personalization and defaults. However, the control is exercised by a parent/guardian rather than the minor user directly, and the bill text is truncated so the specific account configuration requirements for minors cannot be fully assessed, warranting a medium tier with moderate confidence.

llm_rationale: The Artificial Intelligence Transparency Act (SB1161) as truncated focuses primarily on amending Virginia's consumer protection provisions (§59.1-200) to add AI-related disclosures to a long list of prohibited fraudulent practices, with new sections 59.1-607 through 59.1-609 not visible in the truncated text. The bill title suggests AI transparency/labeling requirements, but nothing in the visible text creates a mechanism that would change what a user sees in their algorithmic feed — no feed ranking controls, no personalization restrictions, and no user-facing feed settings are established. While labeling of AI-generated content could incidentally affect the feed experience, the bill's primary purpose is consumer fraud protection, not feed regulation.

llm_rationale: This bill, the Artificial Intelligence Transparency Act, primarily amends Virginia's consumer protection statute (§59.1-200) and adds new sections 59.1-607 through 59.1-609. The truncated bill text consists almost entirely of existing prohibited consumer fraud practices under the Virginia Consumer Protection Act, with no visible provisions specifically addressing algorithmic feed personalization, ranking, or user controls. While the title suggests AI transparency requirements — which might include labeling of AI-generated content — no such feed-relevant mechanism is visible in the truncated text, and there is no statutory language creating obligations that would change what users see in their algorithmic feeds. Confidence is moderate-low due to truncation, as the new Chapter 58 sections (59.1-607 through 59.1-609) are not included in the provided text.

llm_rationale: This bill requires public schools to adopt policies restricting student cell phone and social media use during school hours and mandates network-based restrictions to block access to social media platforms on school networks and devices (Section 4). While this limits students' ability to access social media feeds while at school, it does not affect the content, ranking, personalization, or algorithmic curation of what a user sees in their feed — it simply blocks access entirely during school hours. The bill creates no mechanism that changes how a feed is constructed, ranked, or personalized; it is an access restriction policy, not a feed regulation.

llm_rationale: Section C of this bill creates a liability safe harbor that strongly incentivizes platforms to materially change what minor users see in their feeds: platforms must disable engagement-driven design elements (autoplay, infinite scroll, push notifications), limit daily usage to 3 hours, enforce nighttime access blackouts (10:30pm–6:30am), and require parental consent for minor accounts. These are platform-side obligations that directly alter the algorithmic curation environment for minor users. The bill does not give minor users direct individual control — parents must consent and platforms must configure defaults — so this is best classified as 'high' rather than 'direct,' reflecting substantial platform-side constraints on how content is delivered to minors.

llm_rationale: This bill materially affects what minor users see in their feeds through multiple mechanisms. Section B(2)(d) requires platforms to disable autoplay, infinite scroll, and push notifications for minors by default — directly changing the content delivery experience. Section B(2)(a)(3) restricts data collection and sale for minor users, limiting personalization signals. Section D(1) requires parental consent before a minor can change default privacy settings, meaning a parent effectively controls the minor's data environment and thus personalization. While the minor's feed is the affected object, parents exercise significant control over the settings, placing this at 'high' rather than 'direct' given the platform-side mandatory defaults and the substantial scope of feed-affecting provisions (infinite scroll, autoplay, push notifications) that are mandated off by default.

Certain social media algorithms that target children prohibited.
llm_rationale: Section 1, Subdivision 2(a) prohibits social media platforms with over 1,000,000 global account holders from using any social media algorithm — defined as software that prioritizes content using engagement-based recommendation features — to target users under 18 in Minnesota. This directly and substantially constrains what minors see in their feeds by banning algorithmic ranking entirely for that demographic, permitting only chronological display of chosen content or direct search results. Subdivision 2(b) additionally requires verifiable parental consent before a minor can open a new account, adding an age-gating/verification layer. The bill's primary purpose is platform-side prohibition of algorithmic targeting of minors, making feed impact the central mechanism rather than a side effect.

An Act Concerning Minors And Social Media.
last_action: Referred to Joint Committee on General Law
llm_rationale: This bill directly prohibits social media algorithms that target individuals under 17 and bans continuous feeds for that age group, fundamentally altering what minors see in their feeds. The prohibition on algorithmic targeting would eliminate personalized recommendations for minors, and the ban on continuous feeds would remove the infinite-scroll content delivery mechanism. These are platform-side constraints that would substantially change the content environment for minor users without requiring individual user action.

title: "AI Accountability Act"; enact
llm_rationale: This bill (the 'AI Accountability Act') requires Georgia governmental entities to publish AI system usage plans and creates the Georgia Board for Artificial Intelligence to advise those entities and publish model plans. The bill is entirely focused on government agency accountability for AI systems used internally, not on social media platforms or algorithmic content feeds. There is no mechanism in the bill that would change what a user sees in any content feed — no user controls, no platform obligations, no ranking restrictions, and no data personalization requirements directed at consumer-facing platforms.

description: Requires disclosure of social media posts that are paid for by a campaign; authorizes the state board of elections to promulgate certain regulations.
llm_rationale: This bill amends NY Election Law §14-106 to require that paid campaign social media posts include a 'Paid for by:' disclosure label and that copies of such posts be filed with the Board of Elections. It also requires disclosure when political communications include 'materially deceptive media' created by AI or similar technology. Neither provision changes how content is ranked, recommended, or personalized in a user's feed — the bill requires disclosure labels on content and regulatory filings, but creates no mechanism to alter feed ranking, default settings, or user controls. The labeling requirement affects the content of paid political posts, but does not change what a user sees in their algorithmic feed or how the feed is curated.

title: Establishes a statutory framework designed to protect minors who are engaging in the business of vloging on social media and the Internet.
last_action: Committee recommended measure be held for further study
llm_rationale: This bill establishes a labor and compensation framework for minors featured in monetized vlogs, primarily requiring trust fund set-asides and record-keeping by vloggers. Section 28-3-9.4 does include a 'right to be forgotten' provision allowing adults to request deletion of content posted when they were minors, which could affect what appears in a feed, but this is a backward-looking content removal right, not a mechanism that changes how content is ranked, recommended, or personalized for any user. The bill does not alter algorithmic ranking, feed defaults, personalization signals, or give any user control over what they see in their feed.

title: Requiring school districts to prohibit the use of privately owned electronic communication devices during school hours and to prohibit students from using school district computers or devices to access social media platforms.
llm_rationale: This bill requires Kansas school districts to adopt policies prohibiting students from using personal devices during school hours and from accessing social media platforms on school-owned devices. While it restricts students' ability to access social media platforms during school hours, it does not regulate how platforms rank, curate, or personalize content — it simply restricts access to devices and platforms during a specific time and place. The bill creates no mechanism that changes what a user sees in their algorithmic feed; it is a school conduct/device policy, not a feed-regulation measure.

An Act Concerning The Attorney General's Recommendations Regarding Social Media And Minors.
llm_rationale: Section 1(b) prohibits covered operators from serving personalized algorithmic recommendations to minors without parental consent or age verification, directly restricting what minors see in their feeds. Section 1(c)(2)(A) mandates protective default settings for minors including a one-hour daily cap on access to algorithmic feed portions, notification time restrictions, and a privacy-restricted account mode — all of which materially change what a minor sees. Section 1(c)(2)(B) establishes parental control mechanisms allowing parents to further restrict algorithmic feed access and time limits, meaning the minor is not exercising the control but a parent is, which caps this at 'high' rather than 'direct' given the platform-side and parental nature of the controls rather than direct user control.

title: Creates the Rhode Island Social Media Regulation act, which would not permit a Rhode Island resident who is a minor to be an account holder on a social media's platform unless the minor has the express consent of a parent or guardian.
last_action: Committee recommended measure be held for further study
llm_rationale: Section 6-61-3 directly constrains what a Rhode Island minor account holder sees in their feed by prohibiting targeted or suggested content, groups, accounts, and users (subsection 5), banning all advertising (subsection 3), and prohibiting collection of personal information for personalization purposes (subsection 4). These provisions substantially alter the content environment for minors by effectively banning algorithmic personalization and ad-driven content delivery. Section 6-61-2 adds age verification and mandatory parental consent as gatekeeping mechanisms before a minor can even access a platform, while Section 6-61-4 and the limited-hours provision (6-61-5) further restrict the feed environment. The bill's primary purpose is social media regulation for minors, making a 'high' tier appropriate rather than 'medium,' as the feed-affecting provisions are core to the bill, not incidental, though control is exercised at the platform and parental level rather than by the minor directly.

Enacts the "Stop Addictive Feeds Exploitation (SAFE) for all act"; requires a setting which allows a social media user to turn off algorithmic recommendations and other notifications; prohibits the use of dark patterns to subvert choice or inhibit users from accessing certain mechanisms.
llm_rationale: Section 1510 directly requires addictive social media platforms to offer users the ability to turn off algorithmic recommendations, turn off feed notifications, turn off autoplay, and limit daily access — all mechanisms that change what a user sees in their feed. Section 1511 further prohibits dark patterns that would undermine these user controls, ensuring the rights are genuinely accessible. The bill's primary purpose is to give users direct control over their algorithmic feed, making this a clear 'direct' tier classification.

title: Requires warning labels on social media platforms which provide an addictive feed, autoplay, infinite scroll, like counts, and/or push notifications; directs the commissioner of mental health to design the warning label.
llm_rationale: The bill (S04505) requires 'addictive social media platforms' to display a warning label each time a covered user accesses the platform, targeting features like algorithmic feeds, autoplay, infinite scroll, like counts, and push notifications. However, the bill does not restrict or modify these features — it only mandates a warning label at the point of access, with the label's content and design determined by the Commissioner of Mental Health. Section 1521 makes it unlawful to provide these addictive features without displaying the warning, but imposes no obligation to change how content is ranked, personalized, or delivered. The bill may influence user behavior indirectly but creates no mechanism that directly changes what content a user sees in their feed, placing it at the low tier.

title: Social Media Platforms - Vloggers and Video Content Featuring Minors (Child Influencers Protection Act)
llm_rationale: This bill (the Child Influencers Protection Act) primarily regulates compensation requirements for vloggers who feature minor children in monetized content and establishes labor protections for those minors. Section 19–1004 requires social media platforms to permanently delete content upon request from an adult who was featured as a minor, but this is a retroactive content removal right, not a mechanism that changes how content is ranked, recommended, or personalized in a user's feed. The bill creates no user-facing feed controls, no changes to algorithmic ranking or recommendation defaults, and no restrictions on personalization signals — it does not materially affect what any user sees in their algorithmic feed.

title: Social media protections; minors
llm_rationale: Section 18-701(A) requires social media platforms to establish high-privacy default settings for minors, prohibit targeted advertising based on personal information (except age and location), and allow minors to opt out of data collection beyond what is reasonably necessary. The prohibition on personal-information-based ad targeting and the mandate for privacy-protective defaults would materially affect what content and advertisements a minor sees in their feed, since algorithmic personalization depends on personal data. The minor themselves can exercise the opt-out right, though the primary mechanism is platform-side default configuration rather than granular user control over ranking.

Minors; social media and internet safety; account termination upon the request of minors or their parents or guardians; provide
llm_rationale: This bill requires social media platforms to verify user ages and prohibit account access for minors under 14, and for 14-15 year olds without parental consent (Code Section 39-6-2). By preventing underage minors from holding accounts and requiring parental consent for 14-15 year olds, the bill materially affects whether minors can access an algorithmic feed at all, which is a meaningful change to their content environment. However, the bill's primary focus is on account eligibility, consent, and termination rather than directly controlling feed ranking or recommendation mechanics — the feed impact is mediated through access control rather than algorithmic modification. Per the parental controls note, the minor's feed is affected but the control is exercised by parents/guardians for the 14-15 age group, and by outright exclusion for under-14s.

Requires warning labels on social media platforms which provide an addictive feed, autoplay, infinite scroll, like counts, and/or push notifications; directs the commissioner of mental health to design the warning label.
llm_rationale: Section 1521 requires covered operators to display a warning label each time a user accesses a platform offering an addictive feed, autoplay, infinite scroll, like counts, and/or push notifications, but the bill does not require platforms to change, disable, or give users control over these feed mechanisms — it only mandates a warning label be displayed. The bill does not give users direct control over their feed ranking or personalization, nor does it require platforms to alter default feed settings or restrict how content is ranked. The labeling requirement creates a plausible but weak causal link to feed behavior change: users may alter their behavior in response to warnings, but the feed itself is not structurally modified by the legislation.

title: Consumer protection, requires social media platforms terminate certain accounts, display notifications, prohibit certain actions, use age verification, provide certain tools, remove certain content, penalties provided for violations
last_action: Pending House Children and Senior Advocacy
llm_rationale: This bill materially affects what minor users see in their algorithmic feeds through multiple mechanisms. Section 3 mandates default pop-up notifications for minors after one hour of daily use or during nighttime hours; Section 4 prohibits personal-information-based advertising and restricts data collection for minor accounts; and Section 7 requires platforms to provide parental tools to limit recommendation systems, infinite scrolling, autoplay, and ephemeral content feeds, and to allow opting out of engagement features. These provisions directly constrain how content is ranked and delivered to minor users, with control exercised primarily by parents/guardians rather than the minor themselves, placing this at 'high' rather than 'direct.' The bill's primary focus is on minor safety on social media platforms, and the feed-affecting provisions are central to its purpose.

title: Certain social media algorithms targeting children prohibition provision
last_action: Referred to Commerce and Consumer Protection
llm_rationale: Subdivision 2(a) directly prohibits social media platforms from using algorithmic recommendation features to target content at users under 18, effectively mandating that minors receive only chronological or explicitly followed content rather than algorithmically personalized feeds. This is a platform-side constraint that fundamentally changes what minor users see — not a transparency or study measure. Subdivision 2(b) additionally requires verifiable parental consent before minors can open accounts, adding an age-gating/verification layer. The bill's primary purpose is to eliminate algorithmic feed personalization for minors on covered platforms, making this a 'high' tier classification rather than 'direct' because control is exercised at the platform level rather than by the user themselves.

title: California AI Transparency Act.
last_action: Chaptered by Secretary of State - Chapter 674, Statutes of 2025.
llm_rationale: This bill (AB 853) primarily addresses AI content provenance and transparency — requiring large online platforms to detect, preserve, and disclose provenance data embedded in content, and requiring AI systems and capture devices to embed latent disclosures. Section 22757.3.1 requires platforms to provide a user interface disclosing authenticity/origin information about content, but this is a labeling/disclosure mechanism, not a feed ranking or personalization control. The bill does not restrict how platforms rank or recommend content, does not give users control over their algorithmic feeds, and does not alter default feed personalization — it only requires disclosure of content provenance metadata, which has at most a marginal indirect effect on what users see in their feeds.

This directly constrains how platforms may rank, recommend, or surface content to minors, constituting a platform-side restriction on feed curation. Relating to user reports of explicit deep fake material on social media platforms.
llm_rationale: Section 120.102(b) requires social media platforms to remove explicit deep fake material upon user complaint, and Section 120.1025(b) requires platforms to implement measures preventing the same material from being reposted. This does materially affect what users see in their feed by mandating removal of a specific content category, but the mechanism is reactive (triggered by user complaint) rather than a proactive feed ranking or personalization constraint. The bill does not address algorithmic ranking, recommendation signals, or default feed configurations — it is primarily a content moderation and complaint-system bill focused on a narrow harm category, making the feed impact real but indirect and marginal.

Neither provision creates any mechanism that would change what a user sees in their algorithmic feed — there are no user controls, platform design requirements, ranking restrictions, or data limitations imposed on social media platforms. title: Personal rights: liability: social media platforms.
last_action: In Senate. Consideration of Governor's veto pending.
llm_rationale: SB 771 creates civil liability for social media platforms that violate California civil rights statutes (Sections 51.7, 51.9, 52, 52.1) 'through its algorithms that relay content to users,' and deems platforms to have actual knowledge of how their algorithms deliver content. This creates a legal incentive for platforms to modify their algorithmic recommendation systems to avoid amplifying content that constitutes threats, violence, or intimidation based on protected characteristics. However, the bill does not directly mandate any specific feed design, user control, or default setting — it works indirectly through civil liability exposure, which may cause platforms to suppress certain categories of harmful content in feeds. The causal path to feed change is real but indirect, placing this at 'low' rather than 'high.'

description: An act relating to the regulation of social media platforms and artificial intelligence systems
last_action: Read first time and referred to the Committee on Commerce and Economic Development
llm_rationale: Section 4193c imposes platform-side obligations that directly affect what minor users see: platforms must not use design features that encourage excessive/compulsive use (§4193c(1)), must configure all default privacy settings to a high level of privacy (§4193c(2)), must not use dark patterns (§4193c(5)), and must not process a minor's personal data for targeted advertising (§4193c(8)). These provisions collectively constrain how platforms can curate and personalize content for minors, changing the default content environment and feed behavior. While users do not have direct individual controls, the platform-side obligations substantially restrict how content is ranked, recommended, and personalized for minors.

Section 143A.005(c) specifically prohibits platforms from using their own commentary to 'delay or otherwise diminish the visibility of a user's expression,' which could indirectly affect feed ranking. The feed impact is an indirect consequence of the viewpoint-neutrality requirement rather than the bill's primary mechanism.

Social Media Tools for Minor Users & Parents
last_action: House Committee on Appropriations Lay Over Unamended - Amendment(s) Failed
llm_rationale: The bill requires social media companies to implement age assurance systems, provide minor users with tools to control their own experience (including control over 'covered features' such as personalized recommendation systems, auto-play, infinite scroll, and notification features), and provide parents with tools to support minor users. Section 6-1-1601(5) defines 'covered features' to include personalized recommendation systems explicitly defined as systems that 'suggest, promote, or rank content... based on the personal data of users,' directly implicating feed content. The bill also prohibits platforms from using manipulative design or algorithms to increase or sustain minor engagement, which would constrain how platforms curate and rank content for minors. While both minors and parents receive controls, the bill provides direct user-side controls to minors themselves, justifying a 'high' rather than 'medium' classification.

Exempts certain personal information and entities from certain requirements concerning notification and disclosure of personal data.
llm_rationale: This bill amends New Jersey's data privacy law (P.L.2023, c.266) to exempt certain entities and categories of personal data — including insurance institutions, insurance-support organizations, financial institutions, consumer reporting agencies, and national securities associations — from the state's data privacy notification and disclosure requirements. It creates no user-facing controls over algorithmic feeds, does not restrict how content is ranked or recommended, and does not require platforms to change default feed behavior. The bill's effect is to narrow the scope of an existing data privacy law, which has only a highly attenuated and speculative connection to feed personalization.

title: An Act to Regulate Social Media Use by Minors Under 16 Years of Age
llm_rationale: Sections 1500-AA and 1500-BB prohibit children under 14 from accessing social media platforms entirely and require verified parental consent for 14-15 year olds to become or remain account holders. This materially affects what minors see in their feed by either blocking access entirely or conditioning it on parental approval, but the minor does not exercise independent control — the parent does. The bill does not directly regulate ranking algorithms or feed personalization mechanisms, so the feed impact is mediated through age-gating and parental consent rather than direct feed control, warranting a 'medium' classification.

title: Social Media Protections for Minors Under 16
llm_rationale: This bill (§114B-2) prohibits minors under 14 from creating social media accounts entirely, and requires parental consent for minors aged 14-15 to maintain accounts, with platforms required to terminate non-consented accounts. These provisions materially affect what minors see in algorithmic feeds by either removing their access entirely or conditioning access on parental approval. However, the control mechanism is parental/guardian consent rather than direct user control over feed content or ranking, and there are no explicit provisions governing how the algorithm operates for permitted minor accounts, placing this at 'medium' rather than 'high.'

llm_rationale: Section 554I.3 requires parental authorization before a minor can create a social media account, and grants parents control over account/privacy settings and time limits (subsection 5(c) and 5(d)), which would indirectly affect the content environment a minor experiences. However, the minor does not exercise independent feed control — it is the parent who controls settings. The bill's primary purpose is account authorization and parental access, not direct feed ranking or personalization, making this a medium-tier impact consistent with the parental controls note.

Use of social media regulated for minors ages 15 and younger, and anonymous age verification required for websites harmful to minors.
last_action: Introduction and first reading, referred to Commerce Finance and Policy
llm_rationale: Section 1 prohibits social media platforms from allowing minors under 14 to create accounts entirely, and requires parental consent for 14-15 year olds, which directly and categorically changes what these users see in their algorithmic feeds — namely, nothing on these platforms without parental authorization. The bill's definition of 'social media platform' specifically targets platforms that 'employ algorithms that analyze user data to select content for users' and exhibit addictive design features, meaning the restriction is explicitly tied to algorithmic feed delivery. Parental controls govern whether a minor can access the feed at all, classifying this as high rather than direct, since the minor does not exercise independent control but the feed impact is substantial and platform-side.

title: Certain activities regulation by social media platforms
last_action: Referred to Commerce and Consumer Protection
llm_rationale: The bill's truncated text shows its primary focus is on prohibiting deplatforming of political candidates (Sec. 2) and antitrust enforcement against social media platforms (Sec. 3), with findings language referencing 'post-prioritization algorithms' and shadow banning. The automated analysis detected 'chronological order' language in portions not included in the truncated text, suggesting a feed-related provision may exist in Sec. 325M.35 or elsewhere, but the available text does not directly establish a mechanism giving users control over their feed ranking. The bill's main operative provisions concern candidate deplatforming and antitrust contracting rules rather than feed personalization, making any feed impact indirect at best, and confidence is reduced due to truncation.

title: Relating to prohibiting use of social media platforms by children.
llm_rationale: Section 120.113-120.114 prohibit children under 18 from using social media platforms entirely, requiring age verification via government-issued ID before accepting account holders — this categorically removes minors from algorithmic feeds altogether. Section 121.003-121.004 require device manufacturers to enable age markers that notify websites and applications when a minor is using the device, and to install default content filters blocking explicit material, directly affecting what content can be accessed or displayed. Section 3 amends the Education Code to require school devices to block social media platforms entirely. Collectively, these provisions substantially constrain the content environment for minors through platform-side and device-side design defaults and verification requirements, though control is exercised by platforms and parents rather than users themselves.

Relating to censorship of or certain other interference with digital expression, including expression on social media platforms or through electronic mail messages.
llm_rationale: This bill amends Texas H.B. 20 to prohibit social media platforms from censoring users' expression or discriminating in the carriage of expression on the basis of viewpoint, including by 'delaying or otherwise diminishing the visibility' of a user's expression (Sec. 143A.005(c)). This could indirectly affect what appears in feeds by restricting viewpoint-based ranking suppression or downranking. However, the bill's primary purpose is to provide legal remedies (damages, injunctive relief) for censorship violations rather than to directly regulate feed mechanics — the feed impact is a byproduct of the anti-censorship common carrier framework. The explicit carve-out for the platform's 'newsfeed' and 'homepage' in Sec. 143A.005(b) further limits its direct application to algorithmic feed curation.

Protecting children from addictive social media feeds
llm_rationale: Section 2(a) prohibits covered operators from providing an 'addictive feed' — defined as algorithmically personalized content feeds — to any user who may be a minor, unless the platform has used commercially reasonable methods to verify the user is not a minor. This directly changes what minors see in their feed by effectively banning algorithmic personalization for unverified users. The bill's primary purpose, as stated in its title and throughout, is to restrict addictive algorithmic feeds for children, making the feed impact central rather than incidental. Section 2(b) also prohibits late-night notifications to minors, further constraining platform behavior toward this group.

description: For legislation relative to social media, algorithm accountability, and transparency. Advanced Information Technology, the Internet and Cybersecurity.
last_action: Bill reported favorably by committee and referred to the committee on Senate Ways and Means
llm_rationale: Despite its title referencing 'algorithm accountability,' this bill primarily creates a transparency and audit regime: covered platforms must register with a new Office of Social Media Transparency and Accountability, submit annual transparency reports, undergo third-party algorithm risk audits, and meet harm-reduction benchmarks. These are platform-facing regulatory obligations with no provision giving users direct control over their feeds, no requirement to change default ranking behavior, and no user-facing opt-in/opt-out mechanism. The definition of 'algorithm' in Section 1 explicitly references recommendation algorithms that 'passively populate a user's feed,' but the bill's operative provisions (subsections (c)-(h)) only mandate reporting, auditing, and benchmark-setting rather than any change to how content is ranked or surfaced to users. The causal chain from audit requirements to actual feed changes is speculative and indirect.

title: Relating to directing payment, after approval, of certain miscellaneous claims and judgments against the state out of funds designated by this Act; making appropriations.
Relating to the use of safety management software for children on large social media platforms.
last_action: Referred to Trade, Workforce & Economic Development
llm_rationale: Section 509.122 requires large social media platforms to create real-time APIs that allow a child or parent/guardian to delegate permission to third-party safety software to 'manage the online interactions, content, and account settings' of the child — which would include the ability to modify what content a minor sees in their feed. This creates a real mechanism for altering feed content and account settings, but it is mediated through third-party software rather than a direct platform control, and the child's own feed is being managed by a delegated software provider (or parent), not necessarily by the child themselves. The feed impact is genuine but indirect, and control is exercised primarily by a parent or guardian through a third-party intermediary rather than by the minor user directly, warranting a 'medium' classification.

title: Relating to parental controls and filters for certain explicit material on electronic devices; providing a civil penalty.
last_action: Referred to Trade, Workforce & Economic Development
llm_rationale: Section 121.003 requires device manufacturers to automatically enable filters that prevent minor users from accessing or displaying explicit material, and Section 121.004 requires parental controls allowing parents to manage app downloads and usage. These provisions would materially change what a minor user can see on their device, including content served through social media feeds, by suppressing explicit content at the device level. However, control is exercised by the device manufacturer (via default settings) and parents (via parental controls), not by the minor user themselves, and the bill targets devices broadly rather than algorithmic feed mechanics specifically — warranting a medium classification per the parental controls note.

title: Use of social media regulation for minors ages 15 and younger
last_action: Referred to Commerce and Consumer Protection
llm_rationale: Section 1 (325F.6947) prohibits minors under 14 from having accounts on covered social media platforms and requires parental consent for 14-15 year olds, effectively removing algorithmic feeds entirely for younger minors and conditioning feed access on parental approval for older teens. The bill explicitly targets platforms that 'employ algorithms that analyze user data or information on users to select content for users' with 'addictive features,' directly tying the age-gating requirement to algorithmic feed design. Section 2 (325F.6948) adds anonymous age verification requirements for harmful content. Together, these provisions materially change what minors see — or whether they see anything at all — in algorithmic feeds on covered platforms, placing this at the 'high' tier as a platform-side obligation rather than a direct user control.

The primary purpose of the bill is to give users direct control over feed ranking and recommendation mechanics, squarely fitting the 'direct' tier.

llm_rationale: This bill amends Code Section 39-6-2 to prohibit social media platforms from permitting minors aged 14 or younger to hold accounts, and requires parental consent for minors aged 15-17. By barring younger minors from having accounts entirely, the bill prevents them from receiving any algorithmic feed content at all, and for older minors, a parent must affirmatively consent before the minor can access a personalized feed. The feed impact is real but mediated — for under-14s, it is a complete exclusion, and for 15-17 year-olds, a parent controls access — making this a 'medium' classification consistent with the parental controls guidance.

title: Creates the Rhode Island Social Media Regulation act, which would not permit a Rhode Island resident who is a minor to be an account holder on a social media's platform unless the minor has the express consent of a parent or guardian.
last_action: Committee recommended measure be held for further study
llm_rationale: Section 6-61-3 directly restricts what minor account holders see in their feeds by prohibiting targeted or suggested content (§6-61-3(5)), banning advertising (§6-61-3(3)), and prohibiting data collection for personalization purposes (§6-61-3(4)), which would fundamentally alter the algorithmic content environment for minors. Section 6-61-2 requires age verification and parental consent before a minor can access a platform at all, functioning as a gate to the personalized feed experience. These are platform-side obligations that substantially constrain how social media companies curate and deliver content to minor users, though individual minor users do not themselves exercise direct control over ranking settings.

Generally revise social media laws for minors
llm_rationale: Montana HB 925 (the Social Media Youth Protection Act) substantially constrains how platforms curate content for minor users through multiple feed-affecting mechanisms. Section 5 requires default privacy settings that restrict content visibility, disable search indexing, and limit data collection/sale for minor accounts, directly affecting what minors can see and who can see their content. Section 5(5) requires disabling autoplay and infinite scroll features — core engagement-driven design elements defined in Section 3(8) — which directly changes the content delivery experience for minor users. Section 4 mandates an age-assurance system to identify minors and subject them to these restrictions. While control is exercised at the platform level rather than by the user or parent directly, the bill's primary purpose is to constrain how algorithmic curation and engagement-driven design operate for minors, warranting a 'high' tier classification.

title: Require certain application store-based parental controls
llm_rationale: Section (F) requires developers of covered applications to provide parental controls including the ability to 'manage the delivery of age appropriate content' and limit time spent, which would directly affect what a minor user sees. Section (D) requires app stores to obtain parental consent before permitting users under 16 to download covered applications, and Section (E) requires age-signal APIs that enable downstream personalization changes. The feed impact is real but mediated through parents rather than the minor user directly, and the bill's primary focus is on app store distribution and age-estimation infrastructure rather than feed mechanics specifically, warranting a medium classification.

title: To Amend The Social Media Safety Act.

title: To Create A Private Right Of Action Against A Social Media Platform That Causes Harm; And To Impose A Civil Penalty On A Social Media Platform That Knowingly And Willfully Contributes To A Minor's Suicide Or Suicide Attempt.
last_action: Notification that SB612 is now Act 901
llm_rationale: Section 4-88-1502 prohibits social media platforms from using any 'design, algorithm, or feature' that causes users to develop eating disorders, commit suicide, purchase controlled substances, or develop platform addiction — directly constraining how platforms may rank and promote content in feeds. The definition of 'promoting' in Section 4-88-1501(3) explicitly encompasses 'placing content on the feed, screen, account, or other digital medium' including 'algorithmic promotion of the content based on a previous search or engagement,' confirming the bill targets feed mechanics directly. The private right of action and civil penalties in Section 4-88-1503 create strong legal incentives for platforms to change their algorithmic curation practices, materially affecting what content is surfaced or suppressed in user feeds — particularly for minors.

description: Excise tax on certain social media platform businesses established.
llm_rationale: This bill establishes an excise tax on social media platform businesses based on the number of Minnesota consumers from whom they collect data, depositing revenues into the general fund. It creates no mechanism that would change what a user sees in their feed — there are no user controls, ranking restrictions, default setting requirements, or personalization constraints. While the tax is nominally tied to data collection, it imposes no obligation on how platforms use that data for feed personalization and creates no user-facing rights or controls.

Relates to social media open application programming; requires social media platforms to implement and maintain a standards-based application programming interface that permits third-party applications to retrieve data at no cost to be used for the user's benefit and to provide certain information to users; requires social media companies to submit a report to the attorney general.
llm_rationale: This bill (§1121) requires social media platforms to implement open APIs that give users and authorized representatives access to the data used to make 'filtering or ranking decisions,' including social recommendations, timeline content, and personalization signals. While users can theoretically use third-party apps built on this API to interact with their feed data (e.g., adjusting muted/blocked accounts, privacy settings), the bill does not directly require platforms to change how their algorithmic feeds work or offer users alternative ranking options. The feed impact is indirect and contingent on third-party developers building tools that users then choose to use — a plausible but weak causal chain — placing this squarely in the low tier. The reporting requirement to the attorney general (§1122) further confirms the bill's primary focus is on data portability and API interoperability rather than direct feed control.

title: Social media platform businesses excise tax establishment
llm_rationale: This bill establishes an excise tax on social media platform businesses based on the number of Minnesota consumers from whom they collect data. It creates no user-facing controls, no restrictions on how content is ranked or recommended, and no requirements that would change the default content environment. While it touches on data collection by social media platforms, there is no mechanism connecting this tax to feed personalization, ranking signals, or algorithmic curation — the bill's sole purpose is revenue generation through a tiered tax structure.

title: Trade: business practices; regulation of social media use by minors; provide for. Creates new act.
llm_rationale: This bill requires social media companies to verify the age of Michigan residents and obtain parental consent before allowing minors to create or access accounts (Section 5). By requiring age verification and parental consent as a precondition to account access, the bill materially affects what minors see in their feed — they cannot receive any personalized feed content without parental authorization. However, the mechanism operates through parental controls and age-gating rather than giving minors direct control over their feed, and the bill does not directly regulate ranking or recommendation algorithms, making 'medium' the appropriate tier.

Providing for protection of minors on social media; and imposing penalties.
llm_rationale: This bill requires social media companies to verify the age of users and obtain parental consent before allowing minors (under 16) to open accounts, and defines 'personalized recommendation system' as a key term suggesting downstream restrictions on algorithmic feeds for minors. The consent and age-verification requirements in Subchapter C would change the default content environment for minors by gatekeeping access entirely, and the definition of 'dark pattern' suggests prohibitions on manipulative design that could affect feed presentation. However, the truncated bill text limits full assessment of how the personalized recommendation system definition is operationalized, and the bill's primary mechanism is parental consent and age-gating rather than direct user control over feed ranking — classifying it as medium, with the parent/guardian exercising control rather than the minor user.

Enacts the "New York artificial intelligence transparency for journalism act"; requires developers of generative artificial intelligence systems or services to post certain information on the developer's website regarding video, audio, text and data from a covered publication used to train the generative artificial intelligence system or service; allows journalism providers to bring an action for damages or injunctive relief against developers.
llm_rationale: This bill requires AI developers to post transparency information on their websites about journalism content used to train their generative AI systems, and to disclose crawler identity information. It creates no mechanism that would change what a user sees in any algorithmic feed — it does not regulate feed ranking, personalization, or content delivery in any way. The bill is entirely focused on AI training data transparency and intellectual property rights for journalism providers, with no user-facing feed controls.

Protecting children from addictive social media feeds
llm_rationale: Section 2(a) makes it unlawful for covered operators to provide an 'addictive feed' — defined as an algorithmically personalized content feed — to any user unless the platform has used commercially reasonable methods to verify the user is not a minor, effectively banning algorithmic feed personalization for minors entirely. This directly changes what covered minors see in their feeds, eliminating algorithmically curated content in favor of non-personalized alternatives. The bill's primary purpose is precisely this feed restriction for minors, making it a 'high' tier rather than 'medium,' since it is a platform-side constraint that substantially constrains how platforms curate content rather than merely giving parents indirect control.

Age verification for access to social media.
llm_rationale: Section 7(a) prohibits social media operators from allowing minor users (under 16) to view social media without first obtaining verifiable parental consent, and Section 7(c) requires operators to allow parents to revoke that consent at any time. This creates a meaningful gate on what minor users can access in their feeds — without parental consent, they cannot view content at all — materially affecting what minors see. However, control is exercised by the parent/guardian rather than the minor user directly, and the bill does not address algorithmic ranking or personalization mechanisms specifically, warranting a 'medium' classification.

title: South Carolina Beverage Container Recycling Act

AN ACT to amend Tennessee Code Annotated, Title 29; Title 33; Title 39 and Title 47, relative to artificial intelligence.
Establishes the "Missouri Social Media Safety for Minors Act"
llm_rationale: Section 407.3475 imposes multiple obligations that directly affect what minors see in their algorithmic feeds: Section 11 prohibits addictive design features targeting minors (including infinite-scroll and auto-play without time-limit controls), bans targeted advertising based on personal data or behavioral profiling, and restricts direct messaging from unverified adults — all of which alter feed content and delivery mechanics. Section 3 bars children under 14 from creating accounts entirely, and Section 4 requires parental consent for 14-15 year olds, gating access to the feed itself. These are platform-side constraints that substantially change the content environment for minor users, though control is exercised by the platform and parents rather than the minor directly. The bill's primary purpose is social media safety for minors, making this a focused rather than omnibus bill, warranting the 'high' classification.

title: License artificial intelligence risk mitigation organizations
Social media platforms’ treatment of minors and providing a penalty. (FE)
llm_rationale: Section 134.07(3) prohibits social media platforms from selecting, prioritizing, deprioritizing, or recommending content to minors based on data gathered about them, which directly eliminates algorithmic personalization of the feed for minors. Section 134.07(5) requires age verification to identify minor users, and Section 134.07(6) bans targeted advertising to minors. These are platform-side obligations that fundamentally change what minor users see in their feeds — replacing data-driven algorithmic curation with only search-responsive and explicitly requested content — making this a strong feed impact bill, though the control is exercised by the platform rather than the user directly.

title: OGSR/Social Media Platform and Antitrust Investigations
llm_rationale: This bill solely extends the repeal date of public records exemptions for information gathered during Attorney General investigations into social media platforms and antitrust violations — from 2026 to 2031. It creates no user-facing controls, does not restrict or modify how content is ranked or recommended, and imposes no obligations on platforms regarding feed personalization or design. The bill has no mechanism that would change what a user sees in their algorithmic feed.

llm_rationale: Section 7 prohibits covered operators from providing an 'addictive feed' — defined as algorithmically personalized content recommendations — to minors unless the platform either has actual knowledge the user is not a minor or has obtained verifiable parental consent. This directly restricts the algorithmic ranking and recommendation mechanism for minors, materially changing what they see in their feed. Section 9 further restricts feed-related notifications during nighttime and school hours. The bill's primary purpose is to constrain algorithmically personalized feeds for minors, making it a strong platform-side restriction on feed curation; parental consent is a condition for restoring personalized feeds, so control is exercised by parents rather than minors directly, which caps the tier at 'high' rather than 'direct.'

llm_rationale: Section 1511 prohibits minors under 14 from holding accounts on covered social media platforms and requires parental consent for 14-15 year olds, effectively removing these minors entirely from algorithmic feeds. Section 1512 requires age verification before any user can access 'material harmful to minors,' mandating that platforms gate and suppress sexually explicit content from users under 18. Together, these provisions substantially constrain what minors can see in their feeds through platform-side obligations — either by denying access entirely or suppressing a category of content — though individual users do not have direct control over ranking signals.

llm_rationale: This bill primarily amends Virginia's Consumer Data Protection Act to add provisions about social media platforms, deployers of AI models, and interoperability interfaces (social graph data portability). The bill text is truncated, but the core provisions visible focus on definitions for data privacy terms, AI systems, and social graph/contextual data portability rather than directly mandating changes to feed ranking or recommendation systems. While 'ranking system' language was flagged and the bill could contain feed-relevant provisions in sections not shown (particularly §59.1-577.2 on interoperability), the primary thrust appears to be data portability and privacy rather than direct feed personalization control. Any feed impact would be indirect, flowing from data portability obligations that might affect personalization inputs rather than explicitly constraining how content is ranked or displayed to users.

llm_rationale: This bill primarily addresses AI transparency in two contexts: (1) notice and appeal rights when AI is used to make consequential decisions (employment, housing, credit, healthcare, etc.) under Section 3, and (2) disclosure requirements when users interact with AI 'companion products' under Section 4. Neither provision addresses algorithmic content feeds, content ranking, or personalization of social media feeds. The companion product disclosure requirement (notice that the product is an AI at the start of each interactive session) is the closest analog to a feed-adjacent mechanism, but companion products are defined as one-on-one conversational AI systems, not content recommendation feeds. This bill creates no mechanism that would change what a user sees in an algorithmic content feed.

title: Minor access to social media.
llm_rationale: The bill (IC 24-16) requires social media providers to obtain verifiable parental consent before creating accounts for minors under 14, mandates age verification of all Indiana account holders, and requires that minor accounts be 'configured in a specified manner, including by disabling access by the account holder to specified features and functionality.' This last provision directly changes what a minor user sees and can access in their feed. The bill also requires platforms to give parents a separate password to set limits on the minor's use, meaning a parent — not the minor — exercises the primary control, warranting a medium rather than high classification per the parental controls note.

While the bill references algorithmic feeds as a trigger condition for the labeling requirement, it creates no mechanism that changes the content, ranking, or personalization of what users see in their feed; the label appears before or upon access but does not modify feed behavior.

Requires warning labels on addictive feature platforms which provide features such as addictive feeds, autoplay, infinite scroll, like counts, and/or push notifications; relates to the effectiveness of certain provisions of law relating thereto.
llm_rationale: The bill (S08827) amends General Business Law to require 'addictive feature platforms' — those offering algorithmic feeds, autoplay, and/or infinite scroll — to display a mandatory Surgeon General warning label when users access these features. The bill targets addictive feed design by acknowledging algorithmic feeds as a noxious feature, but its primary mechanism is a warning label ('The Surgeon General has warned...') rather than any structural change to how content is ranked, personalized, or controlled. While the label must appear conspicuously for at least 10 seconds and is triggered by access to the addictive feed or autoplay features, it does not give users control over their feed, alter default ranking, suppress content categories, or restrict platform personalization — it only adds a notification adjacent to the feed experience. The causal link between the warning label and actual changes in feed content or ranking is weak and indirect, placing this firmly in the 'low' tier.

llm_rationale: This bill (the Child Influencers Protection Act) primarily addresses two issues: requiring vloggers to compensate minors featured in their content (§19-1002/1003) and allowing adults who were featured as children to request permanent deletion of that content (§19-1004). The deletion mechanism in §19-1004 is a retrospective content removal right for adults—not a control over what appears in any user's algorithmic feed. The bill creates no mechanism to change feed ranking, personalization, default settings, or user-facing feed controls. While the deletion provision could remove specific videos from a platform, this is a content moderation/labor protection measure rather than a feed-personalization mechanism, and the causal link to what any user sees in their algorithmic feed is too attenuated to constitute material feed impact.

Consumer protection; access by minors to certain platform portions restricted; hours for sending notifications to covered minors limited; default platform settings on certain platforms required; mechanism for more stringent optional settings required; annual disclosure of certain data by covered operators required; violations a deceptive trade practice
llm_rationale: This bill directly regulates algorithmic feed personalization for minors. Under Section 1(b)(1), covered operators are prohibited from serving personalized algorithmic recommendations to any user unless the platform has verified the user is not a minor or obtained verifiable parental consent — this directly restricts what content minors see in their feeds based on behavioral data. Section 1(c)(2) mandates default platform settings that limit minors' access to algorithmic feed portions to one hour per day, restrict notification hours (8am-9pm), and set accounts to a restricted social mode, all of which materially alter the minor's feed experience. Section 1(c)(3) further requires platforms to maintain a parental control mechanism allowing parents to impose even stricter settings on feed access and recommendation exposure. The bill is primarily focused on minors' algorithmic feed access rather than being an omnibus bill, so no tier reduction is warranted; however, the minor is not the one exercising control — it is exercised through age verification and parental consent mechanisms.

title: App Store Accountability Act; civil penalties, civil action.
description: App Store Accountability Act; civil penalties; civil action. Requires an app store provider, defined in the bill, to verify the age category of an account holder, obtain verifiable parental consent for a minor account holder, and share such age category and consent information with the developer of an app, defined in the bill. The bill requires a developer to verify the age category of an account holder with a developer's app and notify app store providers of any significant change to a developer's app. The bill also requires a developer to provide a parental consent disclosure for each of its apps to each app store provider that makes the developer's app available on its app store, and such provider shall provide such disclosure on its app store. The bill allows the Attorney General and any minor or parent of a minor who suffers harm by reason of a violation of this bill to initiate an action. The bill has a delayed effective date of July 1, 2027.

llm_rationale: This is a broad consumer data privacy bill (NJ DaTA) that requires opt-in consent for data collection and processing, grants consumers data access and portability rights, and establishes an Office of Data Protection. While the opt-in requirement for data processing could indirectly limit the personal data available for feed personalization algorithms, the bill does not specifically address algorithmic feeds, content ranking, or recommendation systems. The definition of 'profiling' (automated decision making to evaluate personal preferences and interests) is present, suggesting some provisions may apply to feed algorithms, but the bill's primary purpose is general data privacy regulation — any feed impact is a plausible but indirect side effect of limiting data available for personalization.

llm_rationale: This bill amends New Jersey's existing data privacy law (P.L.2023, c.266) to exempt certain entities (financial institutions, insurance companies, securities associations, HIPAA-covered entities) and certain categories of personal data from the law's notification and disclosure requirements. It creates no mechanism that would change what a user sees in an algorithmic feed — it does not restrict ranking signals, require feed defaults, or give users control over personalization. The connection to feed personalization is purely theoretical: even if data privacy rules can sometimes indirectly affect personalization, this bill is narrowing exemptions from an existing privacy framework rather than creating any feed-specific obligation.

llm_rationale: This bill expands 'Daniel's Law' protections for judges, law enforcement officers, legislators, and other covered persons by requiring private entities to remove their home addresses and phone numbers from the internet upon request, and establishes a portal through the Office of Information Privacy to manage such requests. While it restricts what certain personal information can be published online, it has no mechanism that changes how algorithmic feeds rank, recommend, or personalize content for any user. The bill is entirely focused on preventing disclosure of specific personal identifying information of covered persons, not on feed curation, ranking signals, or personalization — there is no plausible causal link to what appears in a user's algorithmic feed.

llm_rationale: This bill establishes a public awareness campaign about the dangers of social media use to minors and appropriates $500,000 for its implementation. It creates no legal obligation on social media platforms, imposes no restrictions on algorithmic ranking or content curation, and grants no controls to users, parents, or guardians over what appears in any feed. The bill's sole mechanism is a government-run media campaign targeting public awareness — it does not change what any user sees in their algorithmic feed in any way.

description: App Store Accountability Act; civil penalties; civil action. Requires an app store provider, defined in the bill, to verify the age category of an account holder, obtain verifiable parental consent for a minor account holder, and share such age category and consent information with the developer of an app, defined in the bill. The bill requires a developer to verify the age category of an account holder with a developer's app and notify app store providers of any significant change to a developer's app. The bill also requires a developer to provide a parental consent disclosure for each of its apps to each app store provider that makes the developer's app available on its app store, and such provider shall provide such disclosure on its app store. The bill allows the Attorney General and any minor or parent of a minor who suffers harm by reason of a violation of this bill to initiate an action. The bill has a delayed effective date of July 1, 2027.

description: Social media platforms; school boards; artificial intelligence systems; civil penalties. Imposes additional responsibilities on a controller or processor that operates a social media platform in the Commonwealth with respect to a known minor's use of such platform, including the configuration of default privacy settings to a high level of privacy, prohibition of discrimination and dark patterns, defined in the bill, and duty to exercise reasonable care to avoid any heightened risk of harm to a minor, as such term is defined in the bill. The bill also imposes duties on a controller or processor that operates an artificial intelligence system, defined in the bill, in the Commonwealth, including a duty to exercise reasonable care, provide clear and conspicuous disclosures, obtain consent for data collection, and implement reasonable security measures for data protection. Beginning on January 1, 2027, and annually thereafter, the bill requires a controller or processor that operates a social media platform or an artificial intelligence system to register with the Secretary of the Commonwealth and meet certain registration requirements. The bill allows the Attorney General to seek additional penalties for violations of the registration requirement. Finally, the bill provides that each school board shall prohibit public elementary and secondary schools, school board employees, and school volunteers from using a social media platform as the sole means of communication with students for the facilitation of school-related extracurricular activities. The bill provides an exception to such prohibition when such use is required to meet an objective that cannot be reasonably achieved without such use, provided that the division superintendent or his designee (i) provides clear, written instructions on such use and (ii) may revoke such exception at any time.
last_action: Referred to Committee on Education and Health
llm_rationale: The enacted bill text (§ 22.1-79.3:2) solely prohibits school boards, employees, and volunteers from using social media platforms as the *sole* means of communication with students for extracurricular activities. This is a school communications policy, not a feed regulation — it creates no mechanism that changes how content is ranked, recommended, or personalized for any user. The description references default privacy settings and AI duties, but those provisions are absent from the enrolled bill text, which addresses only school communication practices.

llm_rationale: Section 2 prohibits social media companies from permitting Oklahoma users under age 16 to be account holders, requiring age verification before granting access. This directly and substantially changes what minors see in their algorithmic feeds — they are excluded entirely from covered platforms rather than receiving a modified feed experience. The definition of 'social media platform' in Section 1(11) explicitly includes services that 'employ algorithms that analyze user data or information on users to select content for users,' confirming this bill targets algorithmically-curated feed environments. The bill materially affects the feed by removing access for the covered age group entirely, which is a stronger intervention than merely modifying feed defaults or ranking signals.

llm_rationale: This bill amends New York's WARN Act to require employers to disclose whether mass layoffs are caused by AI or automation, and establishes a pilot program to track such data. The bill does not give users direct control over their feed ranking, restrict how platforms rank organic content, or mandate changes to default feed behavior — it targets paid advertising fraud specifically, making the causal link to feed content indirect and limited.
