extends the accident prevention course internet technology pilot program (Part B); requires applicants complete a motorcycle rider safety course prior to receiving a class M license (Part C); establishes a pilot program requiring the installation of intelligent speed assistance devices for repeated violations of maximum speed limits in the city of New York (Part D); authorizes for-hire autonomous vehicles outside of New York city; extends provisions of law relating thereto (Part E); creates certain crimes against highway workers and intrusion into a work zone; establishes penalties for such crimes including license suspension (Part F); expands the automated work zone speed enforcement program utilizing photo speed violation monitoring systems to include all New York highways (Part G); extends provisions of law relating to certain tax increment financing provisions (Part H); authorizes the MTA to conduct environmental reviews under SEQRA for the crosstown extension of the Second Avenue subway project in two stages (Part I); enacts the dairy promotion act; enacts provisions related to the marketing of agricultural products in New York state; repeals certain provisions of the urban development corporation act relating thereto (Part J); extends the refundability of the investment tax credit for farmers (Part K); relates to the green jobs-green New York program (Part L); authorizes the New York state energy research and development authority to finance a portion of its research, development and demonstration, policy and planning, and Fuel NY program, as well as climate change related expenses of the department of environmental conservation from an assessment on gas and electric corporations (Part M); requires gas, electric, steam and water-works corporations to provide an executive compensation disclosure (Part N); authorizes the public service commission to establish a multiyear rate plan that sets rates on an annual basis for a period of up to 2 years; makes related provisions (Part O); establishes an energy affordability index; permits the public service commission to implant affordability monitors in certain gas or electric corporations (Part P); prohibits terminating utility service in multiple dwellings (Part Q); makes reforms to the state environmental quality review act (Part R); removes the statutory caps on rebates for certain infrastructure projects and vehicle purchases by municipalities (Part S); extends the effectiveness of certain provisions of law relating to the powers and duties of the dormitory authority to establish subsidiaries (Part T); authorizes SUNY trustees to lease and contract to make land available located on SUNY Farmingdale's campus (Subpart A); authorizes SUNY trustees to lease and contract to make land available located on SUNY Stony Brook's campus (Subpart B); authorizes the commissioner of transportation to transfer and convey certain state-owned property in Suffolk county (Subpart C) (Part U); extends the authority of the New York state urban development corporation to administer the empire state economic development fund (Part V); extends the loan powers of the New York state urban development corporation (Part W); requires synthetic content creations system providers to include content provenance verification on synthetic content produced or modified by a synthetic content creations system that such provider makes available (Part X); enacts the "safe by design act" related to children's online safety; requires every operator in this state to conduct age assurance to reasonably determine whether a user is a covered minor; authorizes parental controls; prohibits features that subvert the purposes of online safety for children (Part Y); prohibits advertising former prices unless such former price is the actual, bona fide price of the product for which the retail seller offered to the public; provides that violations of such prohibited advertising of former prices shall constitute a deceptive act or deceptive practice (Part Z); enacts the "data broker accountability act"; makes requirements for data brokers to register with the department of financial services; makes related provisions (Part AA); relates to requiring insurers to provide written explanations for premium increases in certain covered policies (Part BB); authorizes an insurer of homeowners' insurance to refile its homeowners' insurance rates if such insurer had an actual loss ratio for each of the previous two calendar years that is below the benchmark loss ratio specified by the superintendent of financial services (Part CC); requires insurers offer at least one discount on certain real property insurance policies and such insurers inform customers of certain discounts (Part DD); places limitations on damages resulting from motor vehicle accidents (Part EE); relates to the timeframe for reporting fraudulent insurance claims and paying claims (Part FF); requires annual reports on insurance for multi-family buildings (Part GG); relates to the annual consumer guide of health insurers (Subpart A); relates to ongoing treatment by an out-of-network provider during pregnancy (Subpart B); relates to accessible formulary drug lists (Subpart C); relates to utilization reviews for treatment for a chronic health condition (Subpart D) (Part HH); provides a premium deduction for motor vehicle liability, comprehensive and collision insurance rates for proof of installation and operation of a dashboard camera (Part II); protects borrowers and cosigners of private student loans by requiring cosigner releases under certain circumstances, certain disclosures, and other protections (Part JJ); relates to extending the policy period for excess profit refunds or credits to motor vehicle insurance policyholders through June 30, 2029 (Part KK); relates to the effectiveness of the New York state health insurance continuation assistance demonstration project (Part LL).
llm_rationale: Part Y of this omnibus transportation and economic development budget bill contains the 'Safe by Design Act,' which requires operators to conduct age assurance to determine if a user is a covered minor, authorizes parental controls, and prohibits features that subvert children's online safety — provisions that would materially affect what minors see in algorithmic feeds through age verification, default settings, and parental control mechanisms. However, the bill text for Part Y is marked 'intentionally omitted' in the truncated version provided, so the specific feed-affecting statutory language cannot be directly confirmed. As a minor section of a large omnibus budget bill primarily focused on transportation, energy, insurance, and economic development, the tier is reduced one level from 'high' to 'medium,' and confidence is moderate due to the truncated text.

Mississippi Social Media Fraud Accountability and Consumer Protection Act; create.
description: An Act To Be Known As The Mississippi Social Media Fraud Accountability And Consumer Protection Act; To Provide That Social Media Platforms Have A Duty Of Care To Protect Users From Fraudulent Advertisers; To Require Identity Verification, Fraud Detection And Reporting Mechanisms; To Mandate The Removal Of Fraudulent Advertisements Within Seventy-two Hours; To Require Quarterly Transparency Reports; To Establish Liability And A Safe Harbor For Compliant Platforms; To Authorize The Attorney General To Bring Civil Action Against Social Media Platforms That Violate This Act; To Provide Individuals Harmed By A Pattern Or Practice That Violates This Act A Private Right Of Action; To Amend Section 75-24-5, Mississippi Code Of 1972, To Provide That A Violation Of This Act Is An Unfair Or Deceptive Business Practice Under Consumer Protection Laws; To Amend Sections 77-3-803 And 77-3-805, Mississippi Code Of 1972, To Prohibit Caller Id Spoofing In Advertisements; To Amend Section 81-29-3, Mississippi Code Of 1972, To Prohibit The Unauthorized Use Of Bank Names; And For Related Purposes.
llm_rationale: This bill targets fraudulent advertisements on social media platforms, requiring advertiser identity verification, fraud detection systems, and removal of fraudulent ads within 72 hours. While this would incidentally remove some content from users' feeds (fraudulent ads), the bill does not address algorithmic ranking, personalization, or feed curation — it is focused on fraud prevention and consumer protection against deceptive advertisers. The causal link to what a user sees in their algorithmic feed is weak and indirect, as the bill's primary mechanism is anti-fraud compliance rather than feed design or ranking control.

Enacts the New York Artificial Intelligence Civil Rights Act establishing protections for individual rights with respect to computational algorithms; establishes protections against the use of algorithms involved in consequential decisions, such as those that impact people's rights, civil liberties, and livelihoods, including employment, banking, health care, the criminal justice system, public accommodations, and government services; prohibits developers and deployers from offering, licensing, or using covered algorithms that discriminate based on protected characteristics or that cause a disparate impact; requires developers and deployers of covered algorithms to complete independently audited pre-deployment evaluations and post-deployment impact assessments to identify, evaluate, and mitigate any potential biased use or discriminatory outcomes; requires developers and deployers to mitigate any harms identified by the pre-deployment evaluations and impact assessments and ensure that any covered algorithm performs reasonably well and is consistent with its publicly-advertised purpose; increases transparency around the use of covered algorithms in consequential decisions, including providing individuals a right to appeal an algorithmic decision to a human decision-maker; provides remedies for violations.

llm_rationale: SB111 primarily requires large social media platforms (100M+ monthly users) to provide users with portable copies of their personal data on request and to maintain interoperability interfaces allowing data export, including social graph data. While data portability could theoretically enable users to migrate to services with different algorithmic approaches, the bill explicitly states in Section 8 that nothing requires a social media service to 'treat imported data differently from any other user-generated content,' meaning the bill does not obligate any change to feed ranking, personalization, or recommendation systems. The connection to what a user actually sees in their feed is plausible but weak and indirect — the bill is fundamentally about data portability and interoperability, not feed control.

description: An Act To Enact The Mississippi Social Media Fraud Accountability And Consumer Protection Act; To Amend Section 77-3-805, Mississippi Code Of 1972, To Provide That No Person Or Business Shall Take Any Action Which Originates, Facilitates The Transmission Of, Or Delivers To A Recipient A Call That Falsely Identifies The Caller's Name Or Telephone Number When The Equipment Or Service Used By The Person Or Business Is Capable Of Transmitting The Caller's True Name Or Telephone Number; To Provide That It Shall Be Unlawful To Block, Spoof, Or Otherwise Circumvent Caller Identification Services Or Call Screening Products In Connection With Any Electronic Advertisement Or Electronic Solicitation; To Amend Section 81-29-3, Mississippi Code Of 1972, To Provide That No Person Or Entity Shall Use The Name, Trade Name, Trademark, Logo, Phone Number, Or Internet Address Of Any Bank In Any Electronic Advertisement Or Solicitation Without The Consent Of The Bank; To Provide For Enforcement By The Attorney General; To Provide A Civil Action In A Court Of Competent Jurisdiction For Any Person Adversely Affected By Any Pattern Or Practice Which Violates This Act; To Amend Section 75-24-5, Mississippi Code Of 1972, To Provide That A Violation Of This Act Shall Be An Unfair Or Deceptive Trade Practice In Or Affecting Commerce; And For Related Purposes.
llm_rationale: Despite its title referencing 'Social Media Fraud Accountability,' this bill is primarily focused on anti-fraud and anti-impersonation measures for paid advertisements, caller ID spoofing, and bank identity misuse — not algorithmic feed personalization or ranking. Section 3 requires social media platforms to implement advertiser identity verification, impersonation detection, and fraud reporting tools, and to remove fraudulent advertisements within 72 hours, but these obligations affect the policing of paid ads rather than the ranking, curation, or personalization of organic content in a user's feed. The bill creates no mechanism by which users gain control over their algorithmic feed, and no platform obligation to change how content is ranked or recommended to users.

Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act
llm_rationale: This bill (the Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act) primarily addresses AI-generated synthetic content disclosure, provenance tracking, and prohibition of deceptive synthetic content — not algorithmic feed ranking or personalization. Section 6 requires large online platforms to detect and display provenance data for synthetic content, allow users to inspect that data, and provide a takedown request mechanism for deceptive synthetic content, which could marginally affect what content appears in a feed. However, the bill creates no mechanism to control feed ranking, personalization, or recommendation algorithms; its core purpose is AI content authenticity and labeling rather than feed curation. The causal link to feed content is indirect — content removal of deceptive synthetic material may incidentally affect what users see, but this is a side effect of a transparency/authenticity framework, not a feed-control mechanism.

llm_rationale: Section 2(d) requires platforms to configure all default privacy settings for children to 'high level of privacy,' and Section 3(a)(5-7) requires Data Protection Impact Assessments to assess whether algorithms, targeted advertising, and addictive design features could harm children. However, the bill does not directly mandate changes to feed ranking, require user controls over recommendations, or prohibit specific algorithmic behaviors — it primarily requires assessment, documentation, and privacy-protective defaults. The automated relevance score of 9.0 is misleading; the bill's primary mechanism is Data Protection Impact Assessments and a Children's Data Protection Commission, not direct feed control. The default settings requirement (Section 2(d)) could indirectly affect personalization, but no explicit feed ranking or recommendation constraint is created, placing this at 'low' rather than 'direct.'

llm_rationale: The NJ DaTA bill is a comprehensive data privacy law requiring opt-in consent for processing personally identifiable information and granting consumers rights to access, correct, and delete their data. While it defines 'profiling' as automated decision-making using personal data to evaluate preferences and interests — which could include feed personalization — the bill does not explicitly restrict or regulate algorithmic feed ranking or recommendations. Any feed impact would be indirect: limiting data collection through opt-in requirements could reduce the data available for personalization, but no provision directly targets feed curation, ranking signals, or content recommendations. This is a general data privacy framework with a plausible but weak causal link to feed content.

llm_rationale: Section 2(a) prohibits social media platforms from using any 'design, algorithm, practice, affordance, or feature' that could cause child users to develop eating disorders, including promoting diet products. This directly constrains how platforms can rank and recommend content to minors, materially affecting what child users see in their feeds. The bill is platform-side rather than user-controlled, and the audit and correction requirements in Section 2(b) reinforce that platforms must actively modify their algorithmic systems — the impact is 'high' rather than 'direct' because the user has no individual control, but the platform-level obligation to alter feed behavior for child users is substantial.

llm_rationale: Section 100.72 of the bill directly restricts what minors see in their algorithmic feeds by prohibiting 'profile-based feeds' (defined as feeds personalized using the minor's personal information) for minors' accounts, banning infinite scrolling, autoplay video, push notifications, and paid commercial advertising in the feed. These are platform-side mandates that fundamentally alter the content ranking and recommendation environment for minor users. The bill also requires default privacy settings at the most private levels and mandates parental consent before any profile-based personalization can be applied, meaning a minor's feed is materially changed without the minor exercising direct control — a parent does. This is a strong, platform-side feed impact targeting minors specifically.

While labels must appear on content posted to platforms, this bill does not alter how content is ranked, recommended, or personalized in a user's feed — it only requires disclosure labels on specific AI-generated content. The feed impact is marginal: a user might see labeled content that was previously unlabeled, but the bill creates no mechanism to change content ranking, sequencing, or recommendation logic. The connection to algorithmic feed personalization is indirect at best.

title: An Act Addressing Innovations In And The Responsible Use Of Artificial Intelligence.
An Act Promoting The Safety Of Minors On Social Media Platforms.
llm_rationale: This bill directly and substantially changes what minors see in their algorithmic feeds through multiple mechanisms. Section (b) prohibits personalized algorithmic recommendations for minors unless the platform verifies the user is not a minor or obtains verifiable parental consent, effectively requiring age verification before serving a personalized feed. Section (d)(2) mandates default settings for minors including: a one-hour-per-day cap on access to algorithmic feed portions, suppression of sensitive content, restricted notification hours, and privacy mode — all of which a parent can further adjust via a required parental control mechanism. Section (c) requires mandatory in-feed Surgeon General warnings occupying 75% of the screen daily and after each hour of use. The bill's primary purpose is minor safety on social media and its core provisions directly constrain feed personalization, ranking, and delivery for covered minors.

title: Protections for Youth on Social Media
llm_rationale: The bill directly restricts how platforms use personal data of covered minors to drive algorithmic recommendations, prohibiting covered businesses from using a minor's personal data 'to select, recommend, or prioritize media for the covered minor' unless the minor takes certain affirmative actions. It also requires default privacy settings configured to the highest level of privacy, prohibits push notifications between midnight and 6am, and bans algorithmic recommendation systems from being used to distribute illicit substances to minors. These provisions collectively constrain platform-side feed curation and ranking for minors in material ways. Confidence is slightly reduced because the bill text is truncated and the social media platform provisions appear in a separate section from the main online gaming framework, making it difficult to assess the full scope of the social media algorithmic transparency requirements.

description: Special property tax refund threshold lowering and maximum refund increase, excise tax on certain social media platform businesses establishment, onetime public safety aid for local and tribal governments establishment and appropriation
last_action: Authors added Kupec; Seeberger; Putnam; Hauschild
llm_rationale: This is an omnibus tax bill whose primary purposes are (1) lowering the property tax refund threshold, (2) establishing an excise tax on social media platform businesses based on the number of Minnesota consumers from whom they collect data, and (3) distributing public safety aid. The social media excise tax in Section 2 targets data collection as a taxable activity for revenue purposes, but creates no mechanism that would change the content, ranking, or personalization of what users see in their feeds. No user controls, default settings, or ranking restrictions are created. The data privacy signal detected by automated matching reflects the tax base definition ('consumer data'), not any feed-affecting obligation.

title: Surveillance-based price and wage discrimination prohibited.
Online platforms required to provide information pertaining to algorithm use, design transparency and user choice required, civil penalties provided, and rulemaking authorized.
last_action: Introduction and first reading, referred to Commerce Finance and Policy
llm_rationale: This bill directly and materially affects what users see in their algorithmic feeds through multiple mechanisms. Subdivision 4(a) requires that algorithmic recommender systems be configured by default to maximize 'long-term user value metrics' rather than engagement optimization, fundamentally changing default feed behavior. Subdivision 4(b) mandates an 'accessible user interface' enabling users to expressly communicate preferences about types of items recommended or blocked, giving users direct control over feed ranking and content. Subdivision 5 applies similar default requirements specifically to minors. The bill's primary purpose, as stated in its title, is to require design transparency and user choice over algorithmic recommender systems, making this a direct-tier bill with strong feed impact on both the platform-side (mandatory defaults) and user-side (explicit preference controls).

title: Surveillance-based price and wage discrimination prohibition
Requirements for social media platforms related to accounts for minors established, and enforcement mechanisms for regulations on child social media accounts established.
description: Requirements for social media platforms related to accounts for minors established, and enforcement mechanisms for regulations on child social media accounts established.
last_action: Committee report, to adopt as amended and re-refer to Commerce Finance and Policy
llm_rationale: Section 1 of MN HF4138 directly targets 'addictive interface features' including the 'display of a profile-based feed' — defined as a feed personalized based on an account holder's personal information — and prohibits or restricts these features for children (age 15 and under). The bill requires covered platforms to estimate user age and, if the user is likely a child, restrict profile-based feeds, push notifications, autoplay video, and other engagement-driving features. This substantially constrains how platforms curate and rank content for minor users, changing what they see in their algorithmic feed. The bill is platform-side rather than user-controlled, so 'high' rather than 'direct' is appropriate.

title: Social media platforms requirements establishment related to accounts for minors
description: Social media platforms requirements establishment related to accounts for minors
llm_rationale: Minnesota SF4696 directly restricts how covered social media platforms may serve content to minors by banning 'addictive interface features' (Subd. 1(c)) — including infinite scrolling, profile-based feeds, autoplay video, push notifications, and engagement metrics — for users identified as children (age 15 or younger). The definition of 'profile-based feed' (Subd. 1(k)) explicitly targets algorithmic personalization based on personal information, and the bill requires platforms to estimate user age and apply child-protective defaults when they cannot confirm a user is over 15. These are platform-side design mandates that would substantially change what content minors see in their feeds, even without the minor exercising direct control.
